summary: Tractate Yoma opens with the Mishnah's rule that the Kohen Gadol must leave his home seven days before Yom Kippur and stay in the Temple's Chamber of Parhedrin, with a backup priest designated in case he becomes disqualified. The Gemara introduces the parallel case of the parah adumah priest, who was also separated seven days in advance and housed in the northeast Stone House chamber. It explains why only stone and earth vessels were used there (to counter Sadducean practice on tvul yom), why the chamber was placed in the northeast (the heifer is a chatat), and what 'bira' means. The daf closes by tracing the entire seven-day rule to a single verse in Leviticus 8 via a gezerah shavah on the word 'tzivah.'

display.title: Mishnah: The Kohen Gadol separates seven days before Yom Kippur
display.oneLine: The KG is sequestered in the Parhedrin chamber with a backup priest; Rabbi Yehuda wants a backup wife too, but the Sages say that leads to infinite regress.
display.shortSummary: The Mishnah establishes the KG's mandatory seven-day retreat to the Parhedrin chamber before Yom Kippur, with a backup priest designated in case of disqualification. Rabbi Yehuda extends this logic to require a backup wife, since the service requires the KG to be married. The Sages reject this on infinite-regress grounds.
display.whats: The Mishnah opens Yoma with the Kohen Gadol's mandatory week-long retreat to the Parhedrin chamber before Yom Kippur. He needs a backup priest in case he becomes tamei or otherwise disqualified. Rabbi Yehuda goes further: designate a backup wife too, since the Yom Kippur service requires the Kohen Gadol to be married ('and for his house' means 'his wife'). The Sages reject this - if you start planning for every contingency, there is no stopping point.
display.hint: Why seven days specifically? And what makes the Sages' response to Rabbi Yehuda more than just 'don't be absurd'?

learning.learnerQuestion: If the Sages prepare a backup Kohen Gadol, why not prepare for every possible failure - including the death of his wife?
learning.coreTension: Rabbi Yehuda applies the same preparatory logic consistently; the Sages counter that consistency here leads to an infinite chain of contingency-planning that cannot terminate.
learning.coreMove: The Sages invoke 'ein la'davar sof' - infinite regress - as a formal rejection of an otherwise coherent extension of the rule.
learning.resolution: The rule is limited to foreseeable, defined disqualifications (tumah, physical blemish). The backup-wife scenario has no natural stopping point, so it falls outside the rule's scope.

takeaway.text: Halachic preparation for contingencies is bounded by the practical ability to terminate the chain. A rule that generates infinite new obligations cannot stand.

reasoningPattern.notes: Classic Talmudic rejection pattern: 'ein la'davar sof' terminates rules that cannot self-limit.

learning.ahaMoment: The Sages aren't saying Rabbi Yehuda's logic is absurd - they're saying it is too consistent. The very validity of his reasoning is what disqualifies it: any rule that works for a backup wife works equally for a backup backup, indefinitely.
learning.learningBlocker: Students often think the Sages are being dismissive. They are actually accepting the logic and showing it proves too much.
learning.memoryAnchor: Rabbi Yehuda's backup-wife logic is valid - the Sages say so by calling it a chain with no end, not by calling it absurd. The moment a rule creates an infinite chain, it is cut off not by counter-logic but by its own unbounded success.

argumentFlow.label: Opening rule
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: Seven days before Yom Kippur the Sages remove the KG to the Parhedrin chamber and designate a backup priest in case of disqualification.

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Yehuda extends the logic
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Yehuda
argumentFlow.text: Also designate a backup wife, since 'his house' in Leviticus 16:6 means his wife - and if she dies, he cannot perform the service.

argumentFlow.label: Infinite regress
argumentFlow.speaker: Chachamim
argumentFlow.text: If so, there is no end to the matter - you would need a backup for the backup, and so on indefinitely.

sourceRefs.note: The Sages' objection: ein la'davar sof

requiresUnderstanding: The KG must be married to perform the Yom Kippur service (Lev 16:6 - 'and for his house')

misconceptions.misconception: The Sages are dismissing Rabbi Yehuda as impractical.
misconceptions.correction: They are accepting his logic and showing it proves too much - the rejection is formal, not rhetorical.

visualizableElements.item: The Parhedrin chamber with KG isolated from his household
Chain of contingencies: backup priest, backup wife, backup backup...

quizSeeds.question: Why does the Mishnah require a backup priest to be designated in advance?
quizSeeds.answer: In case the KG becomes tamei or suffers another disqualifying event before Yom Kippur, so the service is not interrupted.

quizSeeds.question: What is Rabbi Yehuda's proof that the KG must be married to perform the Yom Kippur service?
quizSeeds.answer: Leviticus 16:6 says 'and he shall atone for him and for his house' - 'his house' is interpreted as his wife.

quizSeeds.question: Why do the Sages reject Rabbi Yehuda's proposal to designate a backup wife?
quizSeeds.answer: It creates infinite regress - the backup wife could also die, requiring another backup, with no stopping point.

sugyot.finalRuling: A backup priest is designated; a backup wife is not.
alternateAngles: The Sages' 'ein la'davar sof' is a self-limiting principle: it only applies when the chain of contingency has no natural endpoint.

display.title: Gemara: The parah adumah parallel and the Stone House chamber
display.oneLine: Before sourcing the KG's seven-day rule, the Gemara notes that the parah adumah priest was also sequestered for seven days in the northeast Stone House, using only vessels that cannot become tamei.
display.shortSummary: The Gemara opens by drawing a parallel to the red heifer priest, who was also separated for seven days and housed in the northeast Stone House chamber. The chamber's name comes from the requirement to use only stone, dung, and earth vessels - materials that cannot become ritually impure.
display.whats: Before asking where the seven-day rule comes from, the Gemara notes that the parah adumah priest was also separated from his home for seven days and housed in the northeast Stone House chamber. The chamber got its name from the requirement to use only stone, dung, and earth vessels - materials that cannot become tamei. This parallel isn't coincidental; it is the key to the Gemara's later derivation.
display.hint: Why does the Gemara introduce the parah adumah case right here, before sourcing the law for the Kohen Gadol?

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the Gemara open the tractate by jumping to the parah adumah before even asking where the KG's seven-day rule comes from?
learning.coreTension: The parah adumah case looks like a detour - but the Gemara needs it on the table before constructing the gezerah shavah that derives both laws from a single verse.
learning.coreMove: Juxtapose the two cases before sourcing either, so the reader sees their structural identity and is primed for the derivation.
learning.resolution: The parah case is not a digression - it is the other half of the gezerah shavah that follows in sugya 6. The Gemara is setting up its pieces.

takeaway.text: When the Gemara introduces a parallel case early, it usually returns to link them formally. The parallel is the argument.

reasoningPattern.notes: The parah adumah is introduced as a structural twin to the KG case, priming the gezerah shavah derivation.

learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara's opening move is to establish that two laws share the same feature (seven-day separation) before proving they share the same source.
learning.learningBlocker: The parah adumah seems like a tangent; it is actually the argument in embryo.
learning.memoryAnchor: Moshe prepared the parah adumah kohen for seven days; Aharon was prepared seven days for the milu'im. One word - 'tzivah' - in both passages is the entire legal bridge.

argumentFlow.label: Parallel case introduced
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Just as the KG separates for seven days, the priest who burns the parah adumah also separated for seven days and lived in the northeast Stone House.

argumentFlow.label: Why 'Stone House'?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The chamber was called the Stone House because all actions with the heifer used dung, stone, and earth vessels - materials that cannot become tamei.

requiresUnderstanding: Stone, earth, and dung vessels cannot become tamei under Torah law (they are not 'kli' for purity purposes)

misconceptions.misconception: The parah adumah case is a digression before the main topic.
misconceptions.correction: It is the structural setup for the gezerah shavah derivation in sugya 6 - both cases are derived together from a single verse.

visualizableElements.item: Northeast corner of Temple Mount with the Stone House chamber

quizSeeds.question: Why was the Stone House chamber for the parah adumah priest located in the northeast?
quizSeeds.answer: The parah is a chatat, requiring the north side of the altar; and it faces the Tent of Meeting entrance, which is on the east. The Sages chose the corner that satisfies both.

quizSeeds.question: Why were only stone, dung, and earth vessels used for the parah adumah service?
quizSeeds.answer: These materials cannot become ritually impure under Torah law, ensuring no vessel could invalidate the ritual.

sugyot.finalRuling: The parah adumah priest was sequestered for seven days in the northeast Stone House chamber, using only vessels that cannot become tamei.

display.title: Why stone vessels? Countering the Sadducees on tvul yom
display.oneLine: The Sages deliberately made the parah priest tamei and then immersed him - a public refutation of the Sadducean tvul yom rule - then used impurity-proof vessels to prevent the provocation from breeding contempt for the ritual.
display.shortSummary: The stone vessels were a response to a secondary problem the Sages themselves created. They intentionally made the parah priest tamei before immersing him, as a public demonstration against the Sadducean rule requiring sunset before the heifer ritual. But this risked people thinking purity standards for the heifer don't matter. The solution: vessels that literally cannot become tamei.
display.whats: The stone vessels need explaining. The Sages deliberately made the heifer priest tamei before immersing him - this was a public statement against the Sadducean position that the priest must wait until sunset after immersion (haarev shemesh). But this provocation created a risk: if people saw the rite performed by a tvul yom, they might think purity standards for the heifer don't matter at all. The Sages countered that by insisting on vessels that literally cannot become tamei.
display.hint: The Sages created a problem by making the priest tamei on purpose, then had to solve it. Why was the provocation worth it?

learning.learnerQuestion: If the Sages were so careful about purity, why did they deliberately make the parah priest impure? And why did that force them to use stone vessels?
learning.coreTension: The Sages needed to publicly reject the Sadducean haarev shemesh rule (tvul yom is unfit) but doing so risked undermining the public's respect for the parah's purity requirements.
learning.coreMove: Counter the Sadducean misconception with a deliberate public act, then neutralize the secondary risk that act creates by making the vessels themselves impervious to impurity.
learning.resolution: The deliberate tumah-and-immersion demonstrated Pharisaic halacha; the impurity-proof vessels prevented that demonstration from being read as permission to be lax about purity.

takeaway.text: A public halachic demonstration can create a secondary perception problem; the remedy for that secondary problem must be baked into the demonstration itself.

reasoningPattern.notes: Deliberate act creates a secondary risk; secondary safeguard is built in to neutralize it.

learning.ahaMoment: The stone vessels are not just about purity - they are the fix for a perception problem the Sages deliberately created. The provocation and the fix are one package.
learning.learningBlocker: Students often stop at 'stone vessels cannot become tamei' without asking why that mattered here specifically.
learning.memoryAnchor: The Sadducees require sunset after immersion; the Rabbis require only the immersion itself. Every design feature of the Kohen Gadol's preparation chamber - stone walls, northeast corner, backup priest - is built to ensure the Yom Kippur service works on Rabbinic terms.

argumentFlow.label: Why so stringent?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: What is the reason they were so strict about purity for the parah?

argumentFlow.label: Tvul yom is valid; Sages prove it publicly
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Since a tvul yom is actually fit to burn the heifer, the Sages would intentionally make the priest tamei, immerse him, and have him burn it - to publicly refute the Sadducean rule requiring sunset.

argumentFlow.label: Secondary safeguard: impurity-proof vessels
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Lest this demonstration breed contempt for purity norms, the Sages required vessels that cannot become tamei at all.

requiresUnderstanding: Tvul yom: a person who immersed today but sunset hasn't arrived yet
Sadducean position: tvul yom is unfit for holy service until sunset

misconceptions.misconception: The stone vessels are simply about maintaining purity.
misconceptions.correction: They are specifically a safeguard against the secondary risk created by the Sages' deliberate provocation of Sadducean practice.

visualizableElements.item: Priest immersed in mikveh mid-day, still dripping, approaching the heifer pyre
Stone vessels that visually signal: we take purity seriously despite the provocative act

quizSeeds.question: Why did the Sages deliberately render the parah adumah priest impure?
quizSeeds.answer: To publicly demonstrate against the Sadducean rule that required sunset (haarev shemesh) before performing the heifer ritual, showing that a tvul yom is in fact valid.

quizSeeds.question: Why did the Sages then require stone and earth vessels for the parah ritual?
quizSeeds.answer: Lest people see the tvul yom performing the ritual and conclude that purity doesn't matter for the parah - using vessels that cannot become tamei countered that impression.

sugyot.finalRuling: A tvul yom is valid for burning the parah adumah; vessels must be impervious to tumah.
alternateAngles: The Sadducee-Pharisee dispute over tvul yom is one of several flashpoints where the two groups diverged on purity law's scope and stringency.

display.title: Why was the chamber placed in the northeast?
display.oneLine: The Stone House chamber was in the northeast because the parah adumah is classified as a chatat (requiring north) and must face the Tent of Meeting entrance (east) - northeast satisfies both, serving as a built-in orientation cue.
display.shortSummary: The Sages chose the northeast corner of the Temple Mount for the parah adumah chamber because the parah qualifies as a chatat (requiring the north side of the altar), and the ceremony must be performed facing the Tent of Meeting entrance, which faces east. The corner combines both requirements and serves as a visual reminder of the ritual's dual orientation.
display.whats: Why northeast? The parah adumah is classified as a chatat, which requires the north side of the altar, and it must be performed 'before the opening of the Tent of Meeting,' which faces east. The Sages placed the sequestration chamber at the exact corner that combines both requirements - so the location itself serves as a constant visual reminder of the ritual's two-directional halakhic demands.
display.hint: The chamber's location is a 'distinctive indicator' - of what exactly, and why does that matter?

learning.learnerQuestion: Why did the location of the priest's sequestration chamber need to reflect the ritual requirements of the heifer ceremony?
learning.coreTension: Two independent Torah requirements point to two different directions (north and east); the Sages found a single physical location that satisfies both simultaneously.
learning.coreMove: Multi-requirement convergence: when two halakhic orientations must be respected, select the intersection point and make it the site - then the site itself serves as a mnemonic.
learning.resolution: Northeast corner = north (chatat requirement) + east (facing the Tent of Meeting). The location encodes the law.

takeaway.text: Physical placement can encode halakhic requirements, making the location itself a heikher (distinctive indicator) that keeps the law vivid for those performing it.

reasoningPattern.notes: Two independent directional requirements satisfied simultaneously by their geometric intersection.

learning.ahaMoment: The chamber is not just housing - it is a mnemonic device. Its location teaches the priest the law before the ritual even begins.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may not realize the parah adumah is classified as a chatat, which is the key to the north requirement.
learning.memoryAnchor: Blood is sprinkled toward the northeast corner of the altar on Yom Kippur. The preparation chamber is placed northeast for the same reason: the preparation faces the culmination.

argumentFlow.label: Why northeast?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: What is distinctive about the northeast corner that led the Sages to place the parah chamber there?

argumentFlow.label: Chatat = north; faces east
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The parah is a chatat, so its service requires the north side. Numbers 19:4 requires it face 'before the Tent of Meeting,' which means east. Northeast is the intersection of both requirements.

argumentFlow.label: Location as distinctive indicator
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Sages placed the chamber in the northeast so the location itself serves as a heikher - a built-in orientation cue to keep the priest alert to both requirements.

keyTerms: el nokach pnei Ohel Moed - before the Tent of Meeting entrance

requiresUnderstanding: Chatat offerings require slaughter on the north side of the altar (Lev 1:11)
The Tent of Meeting entrance faces east

misconceptions.misconception: The northeast location is arbitrary Temple geography.
misconceptions.correction: It is the precise intersection of two independent halakhic directional requirements, and it functions as a built-in mnemonic.

visualizableElements.item: Temple Mount compass diagram: north (chatat), east (Tent of Meeting entrance), northeast (Stone House chamber)

quizSeeds.question: Why is the parah adumah subject to the 'north' requirement for sacrificial slaughter?
quizSeeds.answer: Because the parah is classified as a chatat (sin-offering), and the Torah requires chatat slaughter on the north side of the altar.

quizSeeds.question: What does 'heikher' mean in the context of the Stone House chamber's location?
quizSeeds.answer: A distinctive indicator - the chamber's location at the northeast encodes the ritual's dual directional requirements, keeping the priest alert.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Stone House chamber is placed in the northeast because the parah adumah is a chatat (north side required) and must face the Tent of Meeting entrance (east).
alternateAngles: The parah adumah's classification as a chatat is itself debated elsewhere - here it is assumed.

display.title: What does 'bira' mean? Rabbi Yohanan vs. Reish Lakish
display.oneLine: Rabbi Yohanan says 'bira' was a specific named spot on the Temple Mount; Reish Lakish says it means the entire Temple complex, citing David's prayer in Chronicles.
display.shortSummary: A brief lexical debate about the word 'bira' from the Para mishna. Rabbi Yohanan holds it was a specific named location on the Temple Mount. Reish Lakish holds it means the whole Temple, citing David's description of the future Temple as 'the bira I have prepared.'
display.whats: The Gemara pauses to clarify the word 'bira' from the Para mishna. Rabbi Yohanan: it was a specific location on the Temple Mount with that name, used as a geographic landmark. Reish Lakish: 'bira' means the entire Temple compound, citing David's prayer in Chronicles where he calls the future Temple 'the bira I have prepared.' The practical difference is whether the chamber was near a named spot or simply on the edge of the Temple precinct.
display.hint: This looks like a minor geography dispute. What does each position imply about how the Sages understood the Temple's layout?

learning.learnerQuestion: Does it matter whether 'bira' was a specific spot or the whole Temple? What turns on this lexical question?
learning.coreTension: Rabbi Yohanan treats 'bira' as a proper noun (a named place); Reish Lakish treats it as a common noun (a category - palace/citadel - applied to the whole Temple). The difference shapes how you locate the Stone House chamber.
learning.coreMove: Verse-based definition: Reish Lakish derives the broader meaning from David's use of 'bira' in Chronicles for the entire future Temple.
learning.resolution: The dispute is left unresolved here; both views are preserved. Context-dependent: the Para mishna's meaning is established by whichever reading the reader accepts.

takeaway.text: Hebrew words in Mishnaic geography can be proper names (specific locations) or common nouns (categories). Reading them correctly requires knowing which function the word serves in each context.

reasoningPattern.notes: Classic dispute between specific toponym vs. generic category in Mishnaic geography.

learning.ahaMoment: Reish Lakish's proof from Chronicles shows that 'bira' was an accepted term for the entire Temple complex by the time of the Second Temple - making the Para mishna's usage intelligible without needing a specific named spot.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may dismiss this as a minor geography footnote; it is actually a methodological dispute about how to read Mishnaic geographic terms.
learning.memoryAnchor: Rabbi Yohanan: bira means the whole Temple. Reish Lakish: bira is a specific tower inside it. The dispute is whether a word describes a category or a part.

argumentFlow.label: What is 'bira'?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: What does the Para mishna mean by 'the chamber before the bira'?

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Yohanan: specific named place
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Yohanan (via Rabba bar bar Chana)
argumentFlow.text: There was a specific place on the Temple Mount called 'bira'; the Stone House was adjacent to it.

argumentFlow.label: Reish Lakish: the whole Temple
argumentFlow.speaker: Reish Lakish
argumentFlow.text: The entire Temple is called 'bira', as David uses it in Chronicles: 'the bira I have prepared.'

requiresUnderstanding: I Chronicles 29:19 is part of David's final speech bequeathing the Temple project to Solomon

misconceptions.misconception: The dispute between R. Yohanan and Reish Lakish is trivial Temple geography.
misconceptions.correction: It is a methodological dispute about how to read Mishnaic geographic terms: specific toponym (proper name) vs. common noun (category). The reading affects how we understand the Stone House chamber's location.

visualizableElements.item: Temple Mount schematic with 'bira' labeled as either a spot or the whole complex

quizSeeds.question: What are the two opinions about what 'bira' means in the Para mishna?
quizSeeds.answer: Rabbi Yohanan: a specific named place on the Temple Mount. Reish Lakish: the entire Temple compound (as in David's phrase 'the bira I have prepared' in Chronicles).

quizSeeds.question: What is Reish Lakish's proof that 'bira' means the whole Temple compound?
quizSeeds.answer: He cites I Chronicles 29:19, where David uses 'the bira I have prepared' to describe the entire future Temple - showing 'bira' was an accepted term for the Temple complex.

alternateAngles: The word 'bira' (palace/citadel) is Semitic and appears in Persian-era Biblical texts, suggesting it entered Hebrew during the Babylonian-Persian period.

display.title: Source for the seven-day separation: gezerah shavah from Leviticus 8
display.oneLine: Leviticus 8:34 is the source for both seven-day rules: 'to do' is split off for the parah, 'to atone' for Yom Kippur - and a gezerah shavah on 'tzivah/tzivah' then ties the milu'im verse to the heifer statute in Numbers.
display.shortSummary: The Gemara finally asks where the seven-day separation rule comes from. The answer comes from Leviticus 8:34 (the milu'im inauguration passage): 'as He commanded to do, to atone.' 'To do' is read as the parah adumah; 'to atone' is read as Yom Kippur. But why can't the whole verse apply to one law? Because the heifer doesn't atone, and Yom Kippur has no 'doing.' A gezerah shavah on 'tzivah' (Lev 8:34 and Num 19:2) then formally ties the milu'im model to the heifer.
display.whats: The Gemara finally asks: where does the Torah mandate the seven-day separation at all? The answer comes through Leviticus 8:34 (the milu'im passage): 'as He commanded to do, to atone.' Read carefully, 'to do' refers to the parah adumah and 'to atone' refers to Yom Kippur - the verse teaches both laws together. But why can't the whole verse apply to Yom Kippur alone? Because a parah cannot atone. And why can't it all refer to the heifer? Because the heifer doesn't atone. So the verse must split: each phrase covers one case. The gezerah shavah on 'tzivah/tzivah' then ties the milu'im verse to the heifer statute (Numbers 19:2), locking in the derivation.
display.hint: The gezerah shavah does double duty here: it connects two laws and confirms that the milu'im verse points to the heifer. Why does the Gemara need both the split reading of the verse AND the word analogy?

learning.learnerQuestion: How can one verse in Leviticus 8 be the source for both the Kohen Gadol's seven-day separation and the parah adumah priest's seven-day separation?
learning.coreTension: The verse seems to describe one thing (the milu'im inauguration). Making it the source for two separate laws requires proving neither phrase can apply to only one law alone, then using a word analogy to formally connect the second law.
learning.coreMove: Elimination + gezerah shavah: first prove the verse must split between two laws (by showing each phrase fits only one), then formally link the second law to the verse via a shared word.
learning.resolution: Leviticus 8:34 is the anchor: 'to do' (parah) + 'to atone' (Yom Kippur). The gezerah shavah ('tzivah'/'tzivah' with Num 19:2) formally proves that 'to do' in Lev 8:34 refers to the heifer, completing the two-law derivation.

takeaway.text: A gezerah shavah can do double work: it identifies what a phrase refers to AND imports a law from the text being linked. Here it both identifies 'to do' as the heifer AND imports the sequestration rule.

reasoningPattern.notes: Two-stage derivation: elimination argument first proves the verse must cover both laws; gezerah shavah then formally connects them via shared verbal form 'tzivah'.

learning.ahaMoment: The derivation is elegant because it is constrained: the verse cannot mean only heifer (it says 'to atone') and cannot mean only Yom Kippur (it says 'to do'). The two laws between them exhaust the verse - neither can be dropped.
learning.learningBlocker: Students often miss that the gezerah shavah is needed AFTER the split reading, not instead of it. The split reading establishes that Lev 8:34 refers to the heifer; the gezerah shavah imports the sequestration rule to the heifer from the milu'im model.
learning.memoryAnchor: 'Tzivah' before the parah adumah; 'tzivah' before the milu'im. Same word, same position (before service), same function. The gezerah shavah works because every element matches simultaneously.

argumentFlow.label: Source for the seven-day rule?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: From where in the Torah are both sequestration requirements derived?

argumentFlow.label: Leviticus 8:34 split
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Yohanan (via chain)
argumentFlow.text: 'As the Lord commanded to do, to atone': 'to do' = parah actions; 'to atone' = Yom Kippur. The milu'im verse teaches both future sequestrations.

argumentFlow.label: Why can't the whole verse apply to Yom Kippur?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Granted, the whole verse cannot refer to the heifer - 'to atone' is written and the heifer doesn't atone. But why can't it all refer to Yom Kippur?

argumentFlow.label: Gezerah shavah: tzivah/tzivah
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Derive it via verbal analogy: 'tzivah' in Lev 8:34 and 'tzivah' in Num 19:2 (the heifer statute). Just as there (Num 19) is the heifer, so here (Lev 8) refers to the heifer. And just as here (Lev 8) requires sequestration, so too there (Num 19) requires sequestration.

requiresUnderstanding: Leviticus 8:34 is from the milu'im inauguration narrative
Numbers 19:2 is the introduction to the parah adumah statute
Gezerah shavah requires a tradition; it cannot be invented

misconceptions.misconception: The gezerah shavah is the entire derivation.
misconceptions.correction: The elimination argument (proving the verse must split) comes first; the gezerah shavah then formally links Lev 8:34 to the heifer statute in Num 19:2.

visualizableElements.item: Verse diagram: Lev 8:34 split - 'to do' arrow to parah, 'to atone' arrow to Yom Kippur

quizSeeds.question: Which verse is the source for both seven-day sequestration rules, and how does one verse teach two laws?
quizSeeds.answer: Leviticus 8:34: 'as the Lord commanded to do, to atone.' 'To do' refers to the parah adumah; 'to atone' refers to Yom Kippur. Neither phrase fits only one law, so the verse is split between them.

quizSeeds.question: Why can't Leviticus 8:34 refer only to Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: The phrase 'to do' does not describe Yom Kippur actions specifically; without the gezerah shavah, 'to do' is left without a referent. The gezerah shavah on 'tzivah' connects it to the heifer.

quizSeeds.question: What two verses are linked by the gezerah shavah, and what word is shared?
quizSeeds.answer: Leviticus 8:34 ('the Lord commanded to do') and Numbers 19:2 ('the statute that the Lord commanded'). The shared word is 'tzivah' (commanded).

sugyot.finalRuling: Both sequestration requirements (KG for Yom Kippur, priest for parah) derive from Leviticus 8:34, confirmed by a gezerah shavah with Numbers 19:2.
alternateAngles: The gezerah shavah is traditionally said to require a mesorah (received tradition) - the Sages could not invent it. Its use here reflects a transmitted exegetical tradition tracing the rule to Sinai.

glossary.en: The private chamber in the Temple where the Kohen Gadol lived during the seven days before Yom Kippur, separated from his home to maintain ritual purity.
Red Heifer. A completely red cow whose ashes, mixed with water, purified someone who had become tamei from contact with a corpse. Famously one of the Torah's hardest chukim to explain.

A person who immersed in a mikveh today but hasn't yet reached sunset. The Pharisees held such a person could burn the Red Heifer; the Sadducees required waiting until sunset. The Sages deliberately assigned a tvul yom to the ritual to publicly reject the Sadducean position.

Verbal analogy. When the same word or phrase appears in two Torah passages, the rules of one passage carry over to the other. One of the thirteen principles of biblical interpretation; must be received as a tradition, not invented.

The seven-day inauguration ceremony for the Mishkan priesthood. Moses ran a full week of practice service before Aaron and his sons formally began. This is the template from which the seven-day separation rule is derived.

rashiTranslations.en: "Seven days before Yom Kippur they separate the Kohen Gadol" - because the entire Yom Kippur service
is valid only when performed by the appointed High Priest, as derived in Horayot (last chapter, 12b).
Scripture says regarding Yom Kippur: "the kohen who is anointed shall make atonement" - limiting the service to the Kohen Gadol.
The Gemara (6a) explains why the separation is necessary. "To the Parhedrin chamber" - that was the chamber's name.
The Gemara explains why the chamber bore that name.
"And they prepare for him" - they designate another kohen to serve as High Priest in his place,
should some disqualifying event befall the Kohen Gadol before Yom Kippur.
The possible disqualification: a seminal emission (keri) or other impurity that bars entry to the Temple.
"And for his household" - this phrase about the Yom Kippur atonement appears in Acharei Mot.
"If so" - Rabbi Yehudah requires preparing a backup wife lest the first die before Yom Kippur.
"There is no end to the matter" - the Rabbis object: if we worry about death, the backup wife might also die, and so on without end.
Perhaps the second wife too will die - the objection stands. The Gemara now opens. "The burning of the cow" -
"of the cow" refers to the Parah Adumah (red heifer). "On the face of the Birah" - the chamber's location.
"The Birah" - the Gemara below explains what this term means.
"To the north-east" - the chamber was in the northeastern corner of the Temple courtyard.
"All its rites" of the Parah Adumah had to be performed with implements not susceptible to impurity.
"Vessels of dung" (klei gelalin) - implements made from dried cattle dung, which cannot receive impurity.
These three vessel types are explained in Menachot, in the chapter of Rabbi Yishmael.
"Vessels of earth" (klei adamah) - not kiln-fired earthenware,
but hewn from natural soft stone or compacted earth that does not accept impurity.
These are carved from chalk-like soft stone; hollowed-out vessels, not fired clay.
"What is the reason" the Sages required these non-susceptible vessels for the Parah Adumah?
"Because a tevul yom is valid for the Parah Adumah" - the Gemara derives this below,
in the chapter "Taraf BaKalpi" (Yoma 43b), from the verse "and the pure one shall sprinkle."
"So that they not treat it lightly" - since a tevul yom (who immersed but awaits sunset) is valid,
people might reason: since even a tevul yom is kosher for the Parah Adumah,
perhaps there is no need to be scrupulous about its purity requirements.
Therefore the Sages imposed many rabbinic stringencies of purity upon the Parah Adumah,
and this is one of those stringencies. "Since it is a sin-offering [chatat]" -
the Parah Adumah is called a chatat, as Scripture says in Numbers 19:
"It is for purification waters, it is a sin-offering." "And a sin-offering requires the north" -
regular sin-offerings brought on the altar are slaughtered in the northern part of the courtyard.
This does not mean the Parah Adumah itself must be slaughtered in the north -
it was slaughtered on the Mount of Olives, outside the Temple entirely.
"And it is written" regarding the cow (Numbers 19): "opposite the face of the Tent of Meeting" -
during the blood-sprinkling ceremony, the kohen stands on the Mount of Olives
and aims toward the entrance of the Sanctuary, which is in the eastern wall of the Temple.
"So that there be a distinguishing mark" - the northeastern chamber signals to all
that this kohen was separated there specifically for the Parah Adumah service.
The visible separation imposes the Parah Adumah service upon him formally,
since that service is called a chatat and requires an eastern orientation; the kohen must
focus his heart on performing all its rites carefully and in the correct sequence.
"The Bira which I prepared" - David's statement in Chronicles about the Temple site.
David designated the threshing-floor of Aravna the Jebusite as the site for the Temple,
and it is King David speaking this verse in Chronicles.
"From where are these matters derived?" - what is the scriptural source for the seven-day separation?
"As he did" - just as Moses separated Aaron and his sons for seven days before the inaugural service of the Mishkan.
At the inauguration (milu'im), God commanded them to remain separated from their homes for seven days.
As Leviticus 8 states: they were to remain at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting
"day and night" - the full seven days of the inauguration period.
On the eighth day they performed the actual inaugural service, as Leviticus 9 records.
"On the eighth day [Moses] called" (Leviticus 9) and then: "approach the altar" - service followed seven days of preparation.
"As he commanded" - this pattern of seven-day separation before a major service applies to all future generations.
"To do" - to perform. The separation of seven days is required before the one-day service of Yom Kippur
and before the Parah Adumah ceremony. "Before the performance" means: the preparatory period precedes the service.
"But say [rather]" - the Gemara introduces an objection to this derivation.
